signal: Vision-language-action models that connect perception, instructions, memory, and robot motion.
whyItMatters: This is the software layer that may let robots generalize across tasks instead of only repeating fixed routines.

signal: Robots train and test in simulated environments before risky real-world deployment.
whyItMatters: Simulation lowers cost, improves safety, and creates scalable training loops for physical tasks.

signal: Human operators generate training data, supervise edge cases, and bridge the gap before full autonomy.
whyItMatters: Most useful robots will likely scale through human-in-the-loop systems before they become highly autonomous.

signal: Motors, joints, hands, batteries, balance, and manipulation determine whether AI can do useful physical work.
whyItMatters: Physical AI fails if the body cannot safely move, lift, grasp, balance, or operate for enough time.

signal: Cameras, depth, force, audio, lidar, tactile sensing, and spatial memory help robots understand physical scenes.
whyItMatters: Robots need reliable perception before they can move near people, objects, tools, and changing environments.

signal: Warehouses, factories, inspection routes, retail back rooms, labs, and homes define how fast robots can become useful.
whyItMatters: Structured environments create the first real markets for physical AI before fully open-world robots mature.
